text: The modern periodic law states that properties of elements are periodic functions of their:
explanation: Moseley's Modern Periodic Law: Properties are periodic functions of atomic number (not mass as Mendeleev stated).
name: Modern Periodic Law

text: Atomic radius across a period (left to right) generally:
explanation: Atomic radius decreases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge (more protons attract electrons more strongly).
name: Atomic Radius Trend

text: Ionisation energy across a period generally:
explanation: IE increases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge and decreasing atomic radius. Noble gases have highest IE.
name: Ionisation Energy Trend

explanation: Electronegativity: F(4.0) > O(3.5) > N(3.0) > Cl(3.0). F is the most electronegative element.
name: Electronegativity Order

text: Electron affinity is the energy change when:
options: A neutral atom loses an electron
A neutral gaseous atom gains one electron
explanation: Electron affinity (EA): energy released when a neutral gaseous atom gains an electron to form anion. Cl has highest EA.
name: Electron Affinity

text: The element with the highest first ionisation energy is:
explanation: Noble gases have highest IE in their period. He has the highest first IE of all elements (~2372 kJ/mol).
name: Highest Ionisation Energy

explanation: Diagonal relationship: Li-Mg, Be-Al, B-Si. Elements show similarity with the element diagonally below-right due to similar charge/size ratio.
name: Diagonal Relationship

text: Which block contains the transition elements?
explanation: d-block elements (Groups 3-12) are transition metals. f-block: lanthanides and actinides (inner transition elements).
name: d-block Elements

text: The shielding effect is maximum for which electrons?
explanation: s-electrons have maximum penetration to nucleus and thus maximum shielding effect. Shielding: s > p > d > f.
name: Shielding Effect

text: The trend in ionic radius for isoelectronic species (same electron configuration) with increasing nuclear charge:
explanation: For isoelectronic species (e.g., N³⁻, O²⁻, F⁻, Na⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺): higher nuclear charge → smaller ionic radius.
name: Isoelectronic Ionic Radius

text: Which of the following has the smallest size?
explanation: All are isoelectronic (10 electrons). Higher nuclear charge → smaller size. Al³⁺ (Z=13) smallest, Na (Z=11) largest among ions.
name: Smallest Isoelectronic Size

text: The second ionisation energy is always greater than the first because:
options: Second electron is in higher shell
Removing electron from positive ion requires more energy
explanation: After removing first electron, the cation has higher effective nuclear charge per electron → second IE > first IE always.
name: Second vs First IE

explanation: Metallic character increases down a group (easier to lose electrons due to larger size, lower IE). Cs is most metallic alkali metal.
name: Metallic Character Trend

text: The element with electronic configuration \( [\text{Xe}]\,4f^{14}5d^{10}6s^2 \) belongs to:
explanation: The last electron fills 5d orbital (d-block). This is Mercury (Hg), Z=80. d-block: last electron in d subshell.
name: Block from Configuration

text: Periodicity in properties of elements is due to:
options: Same physical state at room temperature
Similar electronic configurations of valence shells at regular intervals
explanation: Periodicity arises because elements at regular intervals have similar valence shell electronic configurations, leading to similar properties.
name: Cause of Periodicity
